beats.text: Bayesian inference treats a parameter as a belief that data update, not as a fixed unknown.

visual.sub: A learning rule from conditional probability

beats.text: Bayes' theorem combines a prior belief with the data likelihood to produce the posterior, divided by the evidence.
The evidence is just a normalizing constant, so for inference you keep posterior proportional to likelihood times prior.

visual.note: The form to keep in your head

beats.text: With little data the prior dominates; as observations accumulate the likelihood pulls the posterior toward the truth.
Inference is sequential, since yesterday's posterior becomes today's prior whether data arrive in a batch or a stream.
Both camps accept the same equation; they disagree only on what a probability actually means.
A conjugate prior keeps prior and posterior in the same family, turning the hard integral into simple arithmetic.
For a Beta prior and Binomial data you add the successes to alpha and the failures to beta.
Start from a Beta(2,2) prior, observe seven heads in ten flips, and the posterior becomes Beta(9,5).
The posterior mean of nine over fourteen is about 0.643, shrunk below the raw MLE of 0.70 toward a fair coin.
The Normal-Normal update is just as clean in precision: posterior precision equals prior precision plus data precision.
Each observation adds one over sigma squared of precision, so the posterior tightens like one over n.

concepts.title: MAP vs MLE: two ways to pick one number

beats.text: When engineering needs a single estimate, two point estimators dominate: maximum likelihood and maximum a posteriori.

visual.sub: Collapsing a posterior to one number

beats.text: MLE maximizes the data likelihood alone, while MAP maximizes the likelihood times the prior.

visual.note: MLE with the prior multiplied back in

beats.text: MAP collapses exactly to MLE when the prior is flat, so MLE is the case of refusing to state a prior.
Regularization is a prior: an L2 penalty is MAP under a Gaussian prior, and L1 is a Laplace prior.
For the Beta posterior the MLE, MAP mode, and posterior mean all have closed forms that differ on small data.
At n equal to three the three estimators sit far apart; by large n they march together.
Both frameworks produce an interval, but they answer two genuinely different questions.
A credible interval is a direct probability statement: given this data, theta lies inside it with probability 0.95.
A confidence interval's 95 percent is a property of the procedure across hypothetical repeats, not of this one interval.

visual.label: of intervals cover theta over repeats

beats.text: The credible interval needs a prior; the confidence interval does not, which is its appeal to a regulator.
With a large sample and a flat prior the two intervals nearly coincide, hiding the distinction until it bites.
They diverge near a boundary, where a confidence interval can stray below zero but a bounded prior cannot.

concepts.title: When to go Bayesian: small data, real priors, hierarchy

beats.text: Bayesian inference is a tool with a cost, and three regimes where it clearly pays for itself.
On small data the MLE is high-variance and can hit the boundary, while a mild prior reports calibrated uncertainty.
When you genuinely know something, a prior is the disciplined way to encode it instead of discarding signal.
A hierarchical model shares a hyper-prior across groups so each estimate borrows strength from the others.
Noisy small-sample groups shrink heavily toward the population mean while well-measured groups barely move.
Stay frequentist when no defensible prior exists or a coverage guarantee against an adversarial theta is required.
